content.text: At the heart of every language model inference step is a simple question:
given a distribution over the entire vocabulary, which token do we pick next?

Sampling strategies — temperature scaling, top-k filtering, and top-p nucleus sampling — are the algorithms that answer that question. They transform raw logits into a controlled probability distribution and then draw from it. Understanding them is non-negotiable for anyone building agentic AI systems, because the wrong sampling configuration is one of the most common causes of flaky agent behavior in production.
What it is
After a transformer computes a forward pass, it emits a vector of real-valued
logits

— one score per token in the vocabulary (often 32 000 to 128 000 tokens). Those raw scores are meaningless as probabilities until you pass them through a softmax function. Sampling strategies sit between the raw logits and the final softmax draw, reshaping the distribution before a token is drawn. Three fundamental controls dominate modern inference APIs:
Temperature
— a scalar divisor applied to logits before softmax.
Top-k filtering
— zeroing out all but the k highest-probability tokens.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling
— keeping the smallest prefix of the sorted-probability list whose cumulative mass reaches p, then renormalizing.
Greedy decoding
— the degenerate case of temperature → 0 or top-k = 1, always picking argmax.

These controls are not mutually exclusive: most production APIs apply temperature first, then optionally top-k, then optionally top-p, in that order. The result is a renormalized distribution from which a single token is drawn using categorical sampling.
Why it matters

Language models generate text one token at a time, and at each step they are asked to make a stochastic choice from a distribution. If that distribution is sharp (peaked around one token), the output is near-deterministic and consistent. If it is flat, the output varies widely across runs. Agentic systems — those that call tools, parse structured responses, route to sub-agents, and take actions in the world — depend critically on the former for structured outputs and the latter for creative, exploratory steps.
Misconfigured sampling is insidious: a model that almost always emits valid JSON at temperature 0.8 will
occasionally

hallucinate a field name or omit a brace. At ten API calls per agent run and a 1% failure rate, an agent fails roughly once every ten runs. In a CI pipeline that means flaky tests with no reproducible seed. In production it means silent data corruption or dropped tool calls. Setting temperature = 0 for schema-critical steps eliminates that entire failure mode.
Benefits
Temperature provides a single, interpretable creativity dial that engineers can reason about without reading source code.
Top-p adapts the candidate set size automatically to per-step model confidence — few candidates when one token dominates, many when the distribution is broad.
Low-temperature settings dramatically improve reproducibility for structured-output steps in agent pipelines.
Stochastic sampling at moderate temperature enables diverse rollouts, which are essential for self-consistency voting and tree-of-thought planning.
Combining temperature with top-p gives fine-grained control: sharpness of distribution shape (temperature) and tail-truncation (top-p) are independent knobs.
Top-k provides a hard ceiling on vocabulary exploration, useful for latency-sensitive applications where you want a predictable decode path.
Trade-offs
Higher temperature increases output variance — good for brainstorming, harmful for JSON generation or function-name routing.
Top-k with a small k can permanently exclude rare-but-correct tokens (e.g., an obscure enum value, a UUID prefix, or a domain-specific keyword).
Temperature and top-p interact non-linearly: changing one in isolation can surprise you if you have not benchmarked the pair.
Temperature = 0 (greedy) can cause repetitive loops because the model always picks the single likeliest continuation, which may reinforce itself.
Sampling strategies do not fix underlying model factual errors — they only change which plausible-sounding token is selected, not whether the model's probability mass is correctly calibrated.
Most cloud APIs do not expose a random seed, so exact reproducibility at temperature > 0 is impossible without running your own inference.
How it works
Temperature scaling
Before softmax, every logit
is divided by a scalar temperature
As
all probability mass concentrates on the argmax token (greedy). As
the distribution approaches a uniform distribution over the entire vocabulary. At

the original softmax distribution is unchanged. Typical agent settings: T = 0 to 0.3 for tool calls and structured JSON; T = 0.7 to 1.0 for user-facing prose; T = 0.5 to 1.2 for synthetic data generation.
Top-k filtering

After temperature scaling and softmax, sort tokens by descending probability. Zero out all tokens ranked below position k, then renormalize the remaining k tokens to sum to 1. Setting k = 1 is equivalent to greedy decoding. Typical chat models use k = 40 to 100. Top-k is a blunt instrument: it treats a flat distribution (where tokens 1–k are nearly equal) identically to a peaked one (where token 1 dominates), resulting in very different effective diversity.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling

Sort tokens by descending probability and accumulate until the running sum of probabilities reaches or exceeds p. Discard all remaining tokens, renormalize, then sample. The key insight is that the candidate set size varies per decode step: when the model is confident (one token has probability 0.95), the nucleus contains just that token; when the model is uncertain (top 50 tokens each have probability 0.02), many are included. This makes top-p more robust to vocabulary size changes and model confidence fluctuations than top-k.
Interaction order

Most APIs apply the three controls in a fixed order: (1) divide logits by temperature, (2) apply softmax, (3) apply top-k to the probability vector, (4) apply top-p to the resulting probabilities, (5) draw a sample. Using both top-k and top-p simultaneously at aggressive settings (small k and small p) can over-truncate the distribution, reducing diversity more than either control would alone. The common recommendation is to use either top-k or top-p, not both.

import numpy as np
from typing import Optional

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
# Core Sampling Primitives                                             #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

def softmax(logits: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """Numerically stable softmax."""
    shifted = logits - logits.max()
    exp_l = np.exp(shifted)
    return exp_l / exp_l.sum()


def apply_temperature(logits: np.ndarray, temperature: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Divide logits by temperature before softmax.
    temperature=0 is handled by returning argmax directly.
    temperature=1.0 is a no-op (identity).
    """
    if temperature <= 0:
        raise ValueError("temperature must be > 0; use greedy_decode for T=0")
    return logits / temperature


def greedy_decode(logits: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Pick the highest-probability token — equivalent to T → 0."""
    return int(np.argmax(logits))


def top_k_filter(probs: np.ndarray, k: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """Zero out all tokens outside the top-k by probability mass."""
    if k <= 0 or k >= len(probs):
        return probs.copy()
    threshold = np.sort(probs)[::-1][k - 1]
    filtered = np.where(probs >= threshold, probs, 0.0)
    filtered /= filtered.sum()  # renormalize
    return filtered


def top_p_filter(probs: np.ndarray, p: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Nucleus sampling: keep the smallest set whose cumulative prob >= p."""
    sorted_idx = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]
    sorted_probs = probs[sorted_idx]
    cumulative = np.cumsum(sorted_probs)
    # find how many tokens to keep
    cutoff = int(np.searchsorted(cumulative, p)) + 1
    cutoff = max(1, min(cutoff, len(probs)))
    mask = np.zeros_like(probs)
    mask[sorted_idx[:cutoff]] = sorted_probs[:cutoff]
    mask /= mask.sum()
    return mask


def sample(
    logits: np.ndarray,
    temperature: float = 1.0,
    top_k: Optional[int] = None,
    top_p: Optional[float] = None,
    seed: Optional[int] = None,
) -> int:
    """Full sampling pipeline: temperature → top-k → top-p → draw."""
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    if temperature == 0:
        return greedy_decode(logits)
    scaled = apply_temperature(logits, temperature)
    probs = softmax(scaled)
    if top_k is not None:
        probs = top_k_filter(probs, top_k)
    if top_p is not None:
        probs = top_p_filter(probs, top_p)
    return int(np.random.choice(len(probs), p=probs))


# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
# Quick demonstration                                                  #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
if __name__ == "__main__":
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
    # Simulate logits for a tiny 8-token vocabulary
    logits = rng.normal(0, 2, size=8).astype(np.float32)
    print("logits:", np.round(logits, 2))

    print("greedy:", greedy_decode(logits))
    print("T=0.3 :", sample(logits, temperature=0.3, seed=0))
    print("T=1.0 :", sample(logits, temperature=1.0, seed=0))
    print("top-k=3:", sample(logits, temperature=1.0, top_k=3, seed=0))
    print("top-p=0.9:", sample(logits, temperature=1.0, top_p=0.9, seed=0))
Architecture

The pipeline above shows the canonical decode-step flow. Logits exit the model's final linear layer and flow through three optional filters before a categorical draw yields the next token. Each filter is independently configurable per inference call.
When to use it

Different steps inside an agent pipeline call for very different sampling profiles. The following table is a practical starting point — always benchmark against your specific model and prompt structure before committing to production defaults.
Agent step
Temperature
Top-p
Top-k
Rationale
Tool / function selection
Deterministic; must route correctly every time.
JSON schema filling
Near-deterministic; small T guards against schema drift.
Chain-of-thought reasoning
Some diversity helps multi-step reasoning find correct paths.
User-facing prose
Natural variation; readers notice repetitive outputs.
Self-consistency voting
Needs diverse rollouts; majority vote corrects errors.
Synthetic data generation
Maximize diversity to cover edge cases.
How to implement

Most hosted APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) accept temperature and top_p as request parameters. Set them explicitly on every call — never rely on provider defaults, which may change across model versions. For open-weights models served via vLLM, Ollama, or TGI, you can also set top_k and seed. The code below shows task-specific sampling profiles using the Anthropic Python SDK.

Notice that each logical step in the pipeline uses its own profile object rather than a single global setting. This is the key architectural discipline: sampling is a per-node configuration, not a per-agent configuration.
Examples

The code block below demonstrates a temperature sweep benchmark. Run this against your prompt before shipping any agent node that emits structured output. The sweet spot is typically the highest temperature at which success rate remains at or above 95%.

"""Benchmark tool-call success rate across temperature values.

Run this before deploying an agent to find the temperature sweet spot
for your specific prompt + model combination.
"""

import json
import re
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

TOOL_PROMPT = (
    "Reply with a valid JSON object containing a single key 'action' "
    "whose value is one of: search, summarize, translate. "
    "Do not include any other text."
)

def parse_json_action(text: str) -> str | None:
    """Return the action value if valid JSON with 'action' key, else None."""
    # strip markdown fences if model wraps the JSON
    text = re.sub(r"^```[a-z]*\n?|\n?```$", "", text.strip())
    try:
        obj = json.loads(text)
        val = obj.get("action")
        return val if val in {"search", "summarize", "translate"} else None
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, AttributeError):
        return None


def sweep_temperatures(temps: list[float], trials: int = 10) -> dict:
    results = {}
    for temp in temps:
        successes = 0
        profile = {"temperature": temp, "top_p": 1.0}
        for _ in range(trials):
            text = ""
            try:
                resp = client.messages.create(
                    model="claude-opus-4-5",
                    max_tokens=128,
                    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": TOOL_PROMPT}],
                    **profile,
                )
                text = resp.content[0].text
            except Exception:
                pass
            if parse_json_action(text) is not None:
                successes += 1
        results[temp] = successes / trials
    return results


if __name__ == "__main__":
    temps = [0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0, 1.3]
    print(f"Sweeping {len(temps)} temperature values ({10} trials each)...")
    scores = sweep_temperatures(temps, trials=10)
    print("\nTemperature | Success rate")
    print("------------|-------------")
    for t, s in scores.items():
        bar = "#" * int(s * 20)
        print(f"  {t:.1f}       | {s:.0%}  {bar}")
    best = max(scores, key=scores.get)
    print(f"\nBest temperature for structured output: {best}")

A typical result: temperature 0.0 and 0.3 both achieve 100% success; temperature 0.7 drops to 90%; temperature 1.0 drops to 70%; temperature 1.3 drops to 40%. This illustrates why agent frameworks default to temperature 0 for structured outputs.
Real-world example
Consider a customer-support agent that (1) classifies intent, (2) calls a CRM API, (3) drafts a personalized reply. Three separate model calls with three distinct profiles achieve the best outcome:
Intent classification
uses temperature = 0 and a one-shot prompt. The output must be exactly one of five label strings. Any stochasticity here causes downstream routing failures.
CRM API call
uses temperature = 0.1 and top_p = 0.95. The model must emit a JSON payload with required fields. Slight temperature gives flexibility around field ordering while keeping values stable.
Reply draft
uses temperature = 0.8 and top_p = 0.9. The customer-facing text should feel natural and varied across agents and sessions, not templated.

A common anti-pattern is using a single temperature value (often whatever the framework default is) for all three steps. This usually means the draft is repetitive (low T) or the JSON is occasionally malformed (high T).
Common mistakes
Setting temperature = 0 but also setting top_p = 0.1 — this is redundant at best and can cause undefined behavior in some backends.
Using temperature > 0.5 on any step that must emit parseable JSON, function names, or enum values.
Assuming cloud provider defaults are safe for agents — they are tuned for interactive chat, not structured-output pipelines.
Tuning temperature in isolation without measuring top_p interaction — the two parameters compound.
Not logging the sampling parameters alongside completions — when a flaky run is reported, you need to know the exact configuration that produced it.
Using top-k with k set too small on code or identifier generation — tokens like module names, variable names, or UUID prefixes may fall below rank k.
Applying the same sampling profile to self-consistency rollouts as to single-sample calls — diversity is a feature for self-consistency, not a bug.
Best practices
Define named sampling profiles (e.g., TOOL_CALL_PROFILE, STRUCTURED_PROFILE, CREATIVE_PROFILE) in a shared config module and import them wherever you make API calls.
Always set temperature, top_p, and max_tokens explicitly — never rely on defaults.
Benchmark tool-call success rate across a temperature grid (0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7) before choosing a production value.
For structured-output steps, use constrained decoding (JSON mode, guided generation) in addition to low temperature for a further reliability boost.
Log sampling parameters with every completion for debugging and reproducibility audits.
When using self-consistency, set temperature high enough to generate genuinely diverse candidates — typically 0.6 to 0.8.
For open-weights inference, set a fixed seed when you need reproducible outputs in tests.
Prefer top-p over top-k when the vocabulary tail matters (code generation, identifier emission, low-frequency domain terms).
Performance & scalability

Sampling strategy configuration has negligible impact on inference latency for hosted APIs — the temperature and top-p computations are trivial compared to the forward pass. On self-hosted inference with speculative decoding, the sampling strategy
does

interact with the draft model's output distribution: aggressive truncation (very low T or small k) can reduce the acceptance rate of draft tokens, slightly increasing effective latency. At scale, the more important concern is:
Determinism at T=0

— most cloud backends cannot guarantee bitwise reproducibility even at temperature 0 due to floating-point nondeterminism across hardware replicas. If exact reproducibility matters, you need a self-hosted inference server with a fixed seed.
Retry budgets
— if you use stochastic sampling on structured-output steps, budget retries in your agent loop. A common pattern is: try temperature 0.3, on parse failure retry at temperature 0.0.
Batch inference
— when generating N candidates for self-consistency, submit them in a single batched request rather than N sequential calls to reduce wall-clock time.
Cost

— sampling parameters affect output token count indirectly: greedy decoding sometimes produces shorter completions than stochastic sampling because the model may not explore alternative sentence structures.
Tooling & ecosystem
Anthropic SDK (anthropic-py)
— exposes temperature and top_p as first-class parameters on messages.create(). Does not expose top_k or seed for hosted models.
OpenAI SDK
— temperature, top_p, and seed are all supported. Seed enables near-deterministic outputs for debugging.
vLLM
— open-source inference server that supports temperature, top_k, top_p, and seed for fully reproducible sampling.
Ollama
— local model server with a simple REST API that accepts temperature and top_p.
LangChain / LangGraph
— pass sampling parameters via the model constructor (e.g., ChatAnthropic(temperature=0.0)).
Hugging Face generate()
— exposes do_sample, temperature, top_k, top_p, and repetition_penalty for fine-grained local control.
Outlines / Guidance
— libraries that combine sampling with constrained decoding (grammar-guided generation), making structured output reliable even at higher temperatures.
Comparison with alternatives
Strategy
Mechanism
Strengths
Weaknesses
Greedy (T=0)
Always pick argmax
Fully deterministic, fast
Repetition loops, misses valid alternatives
Temperature
Scale logits before softmax
Simple, interpretable
Flat distribution still includes bad tokens
Top-k
Keep k most probable tokens
Hard ceiling on exploration
Fixed k ignores per-step confidence
Top-p (nucleus)
Cumulative probability threshold
Adapts to model confidence
Harder to tune; interacts with temperature
Beam search
Expand B hypotheses in parallel
Finds high-probability sequences
Expensive, tends to produce generic text
Constrained decoding
Grammar / schema guided generation
Guarantees valid structure
Requires schema definition upfront

For most agentic use cases the recommended combination is: temperature = 0 + constrained decoding for tool calls; temperature 0.7–0.9 + top-p 0.9 for prose. Beam search is rarely used in agentic pipelines because it is expensive at long context lengths and tends to produce bland output.
Security & reliability
Prompt injection via high temperature

— a crafted user message can sometimes coax a model into deviant behavior more easily at high temperature because the probability mass over harmful tokens is less suppressed. Use low temperature for security-sensitive classification steps.
Unreliable structured output
— JSON parse failures are a reliability hazard in agentic loops. Temperature > 0.5 on structured-output steps should always be paired with a retry-at-lower-temperature fallback.
Audit and reproducibility

— production systems that make consequential decisions (e.g., approving a refund, sending an email) should log the exact temperature, top_p, and completion for every step. Without this, debugging production failures is nearly impossible.
Regression testing
— evaluations that rely on specific model outputs should run at temperature = 0 and fixed seed (if the backend supports it) to reduce variance in CI.
Defense in depth
— sampling strategy is one layer; also apply output validation (JSON schema check, regex), retry logic, and rate limiting for a reliable agent.
Key takeaways
Sampling strategies reshape the next-token distribution before selection — they sit between logits and the final draw.
Temperature controls distribution sharpness; top-k and top-p restrict the candidate set in complementary ways.
Agent pipelines should use per-step sampling profiles, not a global setting.
Use temperature = 0 or 0.1 for tool routing, JSON filling, and any step with a narrow correct answer.
Use temperature 0.6–0.9 for user-facing prose and self-consistency rollouts that benefit from diversity.
Top-p adapts candidate count to per-step model confidence; prefer it over top-k when vocabulary tail matters.
Always benchmark tool-call success rate across a temperature grid before committing to production values.
Log sampling parameters with every completion for debugging and audit.
Constrained decoding (JSON mode, grammar-guided sampling) combined with low temperature is the most reliable pattern for structured-output agent steps.
Mental model
Think of sampling strategy as the
confidence vs. creativity dial

on a skilled author. At one extreme, the author always picks the single most predictable word — correct, but dull and sometimes stuck in loops. At the other extreme, the author rolls a die across the entire dictionary — novel, but often nonsensical. Temperature sets how peaked the author's word preferences are. Top-p says: 'only consider words the author would seriously choose, not one-in-a-thousand long-shots.' For an agent, the trick is knowing which steps call for the careful author and which call for the imaginative one — and never confusing the two.
